TextENGNew: ...With this, have all the pieces been arranged on the board of 1983?
Just lining up the pieces for June of 1983 has been an exhausting journey for us.
But at last, we're at the starting line.
From here, the count finally begins at zero.
...And yet... looking at all these various Fragments,
changeReason: Old translation rendered 色々な as 'colorful' but the word means 'various/different'; new translation correctly says 'various Fragments.'

TextENGNew: I can see just how many sentiments converge around June 1983.
There was a time when I dismissed the feelings of people unrelated to me as utterly useless.
...How terribly wrong I was.
Rika Furude did everything in her power to overcome the fate of June 1983.
She took two measures.
The first was reaching out to the Irie Institute—the organization she believed most likely to protect her from the calamity bearing down upon her.
...Ironically, it was Takano of the Irie Institute who sought her life. But at that point in time, it was the best course of action Rika could take.
The second was ensuring that Keiichi Maebara's family—the Maebaras—would transfer to Hinamizawa.
The Maebara family's move to Hinamizawa was never a certainty.
...It was only on the day of the property viewing, when the father encountered us and was moved by some deep impression, that he first resolved to make the move.
That is why our playing in that meadow was essential—it was the critical trigger for the Maebara family's relocation.
How long it took to uncover that single truth.
Through countless iterations of toggling conditions on and off, I finally identified it: meeting Keiichi's father in that meadow was the key.
Keiichi Maebara is a vital key to shattering Rule Z—one of the chains that bind me.
A fresh, pure, and powerful wind—one with the strength to break through the ancient customs and malicious conventions that twist every event in this village into something sinister.
He creates countless catalysts, and he entrusted me with a crucial insight: that to stand against an unyielding fate requires an unyielding will and the courage to believe.
...But I was not the only one who longed for his arrival.
Members of the Sonozaki family who wished to purge the village of its toxic traditions wanted him here too.
Mion Sonozaki, pained by the unconscionable treatment of the Houjou family—of Satoko and Satoshi—swore that she, at least, would protect them both.
And on the night Satoshi Houjou vanished, she unleashed her fury upon Oryou Sonozaki and made her true feelings known.
Those feelings... reached Oryou Sonozaki.
Oryou herself had grown dimly aware of the village's corrupt customs and wished to do something about them.
changeReason: Old translation omitted 薄々 (dimly/vaguely) which qualifies Oryou's awareness; new translation includes 'dimly aware,' indicating she only had a vague sense rather than full understanding.

TextENGNew: But she knew she lacked the power to change things herself, and so she placed her hopes in a fresh wind from outside.
That is why the lots were put up for sale—so the Maebara family could move in.
Even if I danced in that meadow, if Oryou had not put the land up for sale, Keiichi could never have come.

And Rena Ryuugu, who would provide Keiichi with so many critical turning points,@k was saved by returning to Hinamizawa—after the deep trauma of her parents' divorce had once driven her to terminal onset.
Keiichi Maebara's wish to reset his life and start over from his time before moving—that was the very same wish Rena Ryuugu had harbored a year earlier.
Perhaps it was precisely because Rena had walked that same path that she could understand Keiichi.
That understanding gave rise to a miracle—the ability to perceive events from one Fragment while in another.
...If one could apply lessons learned in another Fragment, humans would never make the same mistakes.
The pieces would make their optimal moves, dramatically increasing the probability of winning the game called June 1983.
In other words, Rena Ryuugu plays a crucial role among the pieces on this game board—without even realizing it.
The formidable Rule Z surrounding the Houjou siblings would eventually be spectacularly shattered by their combined efforts.
Of course, Satoko's inner strength and self-mastery from the inside were also a tremendous force.
Their hands met only because both sides reached out to each other.
And it was Keiichi and Rena who taught me that.
...Mion, who made it possible for them to move here and helped them settle into the village.
That's not all.
Irie, too, is an important piece with profound convictions and vital functions.
Driven by his compassion for Satoko, he wields the greatest influence on this game board—the one being most capable of defeating Rule X, Hinamizawa Syndrome itself.
Keiichi and Rena's determination and awakening showed us that Rule X could be resisted, but they did not defeat it.
To truly shatter it requires Irie—and his unwavering resolve to find a cure for Hinamizawa Syndrome.
I never knew Irie harbored so many deep convictions.
I had always thought of him as rather absent-minded, but he turns out to be an extraordinarily powerful piece.
If we gathered all these forces together... could we prevail against Takano's will?
...I have seen Takano's will as well.
It is heartbreakingly formidable... not something easily broken.
No wonder the fate of June 1983 refuses to budge no matter how hard we fight.
The sentiments I have accumulated pale in comparison to the sheer weight of conviction she brings.
...The difference in the weight of our pieces is stark—I, who gave up fighting and lived by inertia, versus she, who has staked her very life on the battle at every turn.
...Sulking and escaping into the sweet aroma of imitation wine—how pitifully light my piece has been.
changeReason: Old translation said 'wine' but Japanese says 偽ワイン (imitation wine); new translation includes 'imitation wine' which carries a different connotation—Rika drinks a non-alcoholic substitute.

TextENGNew: ...With what I have so far, I still cannot defeat Takano.
To win, I must gather still more pieces.
There was a time when Officer Ooishi showed the potential to become a powerful ally.
...But Ooishi labors under a grave misunderstanding—that the Sonozaki family was responsible for the death of a friend he deeply respected. Until that is resolved, he will never become an ally.
...I don't know how to dispel that misunderstanding...@k ...but I must believe it can be done, and keep rolling the die.
Just as Keiichi and Rena became catalysts for changing fate,@k perhaps Akasaka, too, possesses the power to create such a catalyst.
The curse of 1983 is bound up with the curse of Hinamizawa.
...A curse cannot be broken by inner strength alone.
It requires power from the outside as well.
Both sides must reach out, or the hands will never meet—just as with Satoko.
But Akasaka has never remembered us and come rushing to our aid.
changeReason: Old translation omitted 思い出して (remembered us) from the Japanese; new translation includes 'remembered us and come rushing' which adds the important detail that Akasaka fails to recall them.

TextENGNew: ...Even when he did come, it was always too late.
...Too late.
Far too late.
But if I keep believing and keep rolling the die... will Akasaka's piece eventually appear on the game board as an ally?
The final rule, Rule Y, involves the sinister masterminds who wield Takano as their piece.
...To defeat them, I need an extraordinarily powerful piece.
changeReason: Old says 'a very strong opponent for her' while JPN 強い駒 means 'a powerful piece' (as in a chess piece/ally). 'Opponent' suggests fighting against the person rather than using them as an ally. The strategic metaphor is altered.

TextENGNew: ...The only piece with that kind of power is, most likely, Akasaka—who works at the Metropolitan Police in Tokyo.
...There are still more pieces whose strength I need to borrow.
Unless I gather them all, I cannot hope to match Takano.
The game board is prepared.
But the pieces are not enough.
...There is still so far to go.
